questions: Give me a high-level overview of JWT authentication and explain why it matters.
Walk me through a practical example or real-world use case for JWT authentication.
What are the most common pitfalls or mistakes developers make with JWT authentication?
How would you explain JWT authentication to a junior developer who has never seen it?
If you had to use JWT authentication in a high-scale production system, what tradeoffs would you consider?

messages.content: Give me a high-level overview of JWT authentication and explain why it matters.

JWT JSON web token, scenario without JWT and using session, user types user/password, app goes to the db to retrieve the pwd and matches the password, save the a cookie in the session and usually it has a expire date, this works fine but not really scalable. now with JWT, what if we could login once, do one trip to DB, and create a token, this token can identify the user, with no session, and no more trips to the DB, the is the main idea of JWT.
Walk me through a practical example or real-world use case for JWT authentication.

practical example - access my home-video web app, assume home-video is access only via https, types user/pwd, application checks the pwd, usually stored in DB or a vault. app now generates an access token (AT), and we add this token in a special cookies, there are some special cookies that JS does not has access to it (HttpOnly cookies), so we store the AT there. now user leave the app but comes back a few hours later, and try to access GET /videos, that request has a special cookie that the app retrieves and read, there has some info about the user, what he can do, and also the token itself, app has a private key and verify if that token has being tempered or not, if it all good, the request carry out and server the resource, the user does not need to type the password again, this was seemless, stateless and no trip to the DB or over the wire.
What are the most common pitfalls or mistakes developers make with JWT authentication?

Long expiration date — the JWT token should be short lived. One way to make sure the user does not type the password again is the use of refresh tokens. Another mistake is adding the token in common cookies (not HttpOnly), making it vulnerable for attackers.
Can you elaborate further and give a concrete example?

Common time for short-lived tokens is minutes — 15 minutes for instance. Refresh tokens: when the token is about to expire, we can write some filters that identify the expiration, generate a new token and invalidate the old one. Also, secrets should not be added to the payload, as the AT is not encrypted — it's hashed (signed).
How would you explain JWT authentication to a junior developer who has never seen it?

Assuming the junior dev understands the basics of a web app where the user needs to authenticate, and to avoid typing the credentials we can use some mechanisms. The old traditional way is to create a session of the user, that when he comes back and tries to login with a browser for example, the server knows how to identify that user, however it has some limitations regarding scalability. Imagine if the user can login once and come back to the app without re-typing the password, also the app does not need to retrieve the info in the DB every time, and also not keep any session — JWT is stateless. The token though should live short because of security, about 15 minutes, but we can refresh the token and the user still doesn't need to type the password.
If you had to use JWT authentication in a high-scale production system, what tradeoffs would you consider?
It adds some complexity. If a token gets stolen we cannot invalidate it, not easily.
Can you elaborate further and give a concrete example?

In a high scale the token will be shared across instances, making it very hard to track it down. You can only invalidate a refresh token where you can store it in the DB. There is a limitation in the signature size, I don't know the max size.

questions: Give me a high-level overview of java concurrency and explain why it matters.
Walk me through a practical example or real-world use case for java concurrency.
What are the most common pitfalls or mistakes developers make with java concurrency?
How would you explain java concurrency to a junior developer who has never seen it?
If you had to use java concurrency in a high-scale production system, what tradeoffs would you consider?

messages.content: Give me a high-level overview of java concurrency and explain why it matters.

concurrency in java is thought the class Threads or interface Runnable, it makes java program to run other tasks in parallel, for example we can have a thread for IO while wont block the main thread, so that the main thread can execute other tasks
Can you elaborate further and give a concrete example?

in modern java we have CompletableFuture that already provide several methods to deal with async tasks, its very rich and can use lambda to write simple functions and also catch exceptions more gracefully. with lambda we can compose and combine multiple lambda functions. and with ExecutorService provide a fixed thread pool as one of it API
Walk me through a practical example or real-world use case for java concurrency.

I dont know the syntax, but I can create 2 completableFuture, userFuture, to get the user, for example userService.getUser(userID) and a second completableFuture orderFuture, orderService.getOrder(user) then combine the result in a Dashboard object
What are the most common pitfalls or mistakes developers make with java concurrency?

1. Shared mutable state without protection. Wrong collection choice - Using HashMap / ArrayList from multiple threads without synchronization. 2. Deadlocks, thread A waits for Thread B, but thread B is waiting on Thread A. 3. race condition, Thread A should finish Before Thread B, but thread B finishes first.
Can you elaborate further and give a concrete example?
to prevent a deadlock we need to consistent lock order, using synchronized to avoid B get the lock before it should.
How would you explain java concurrency to a junior developer who has never seen it?
Java concurrency is complex, you need to read the fundamentals, start simple examples, read about deadlocks, race condition, then study the Java modern library
If you had to use java concurrency in a high-scale production system, what tradeoffs would you consider?

using threads add more complexity, dealing with async code is not as simple as sync code, but we need threads in a high-scale system, threads make it possible your system to execute tasks seemingly, for example it can handle different tasks like calling an API and the main thread wont be blocked.

evaluations.question: Give me a high-level overview of java concurrency and explain why it matters.
evaluations.answer: concurrency in java is thought the class Threads or interface Runnable, it makes java program to run other tasks in parallel, for example we can have a thread for IO while wont block the main thread, so that the main thread can execute other tasks
evaluations.strongAnswer:
Java concurrency is about coordinating multiple threads that share heap memory so work can progress without each task blocking the whole process.
What matters is not only parallel work, but correctness under the Java Memory Model: atomicity, visibility, ordering, and deadlock avoidance.
In practice, prefer ExecutorService, concurrent collections, immutability, and higher-level primitives over managing raw threads directly.
evaluations.feedback: Adequate, but could go deeper on key points.
evaluations.followUpQuestion: Can you elaborate further and give a concrete example?

evaluations.question: Can you elaborate further and give a concrete example?
evaluations.answer: in modern java we have CompletableFuture that already provide several methods to deal with async tasks, its very rich and can use lambda to write simple functions and also catch exceptions more gracefully. with lambda we can compose and combine multiple lambda functions. and with ExecutorService provide a fixed thread pool as one of it API
evaluations.strongAnswer:
Example: a request needs user data, orders, and recommendations; run those calls in parallel with CompletableFuture on a bounded ExecutorService, then combine the results.
That reduces end-to-end latency because independent I/O waits overlap instead of running serially.
The safe design still needs timeouts, exception handling, and a thread-pool size chosen for the workload.
evaluations.feedback: Adequate, but could go deeper on key points.
evaluations.followUpQuestion: Can you elaborate further and give a concrete example?

evaluations.question: Walk me through a practical example or real-world use case for java concurrency.
evaluations.answer: I dont know the syntax, but I can create 2 completableFuture, userFuture, to get the user, for example userService.getUser(userID) and a second completableFuture orderFuture, orderService.getOrder(user) then combine the result in a Dashboard object
evaluations.strongAnswer:
A common case is a dashboard endpoint that needs profile, orders, and notifications from separate services.
Use CompletableFuture.supplyAsync for each independent call, combine them with allOf or thenCombine, and return once all results are ready.
Run this on a bounded executor, propagate failures clearly, and avoid blocking joins deep inside worker threads.
evaluations.feedback: Adequate, but could go deeper on key points.
evaluations.followUpQuestion: Can you elaborate further and give a concrete example?

evaluations.question: What are the most common pitfalls or mistakes developers make with java concurrency?
evaluations.answer: 1. Shared mutable state without protection. Wrong collection choice - Using HashMap / ArrayList from multiple threads without synchronization. 2. Deadlocks, thread A waits for Thread B, but thread B is waiting on Thread A. 3. race condition, Thread A should finish Before Thread B, but thread B finishes first.
evaluations.strongAnswer:
The biggest mistakes are unsafely sharing mutable state, assuming volatile makes compound operations atomic, and using non-thread-safe collections across threads.
Teams also create deadlocks through inconsistent lock ordering and hurt throughput with oversized pools, blocking tasks, or unbounded queues.
Best practice is to minimize shared state, prefer ConcurrentHashMap and immutable data, and define explicit lock and executor policies.
evaluations.feedback: Adequate, but could go deeper on key points.
evaluations.followUpQuestion: Can you elaborate further and give a concrete example?

evaluations.question: Can you elaborate further and give a concrete example?
evaluations.answer: to prevent a deadlock we need to consistent lock order, using synchronized to avoid B get the lock before it should.
evaluations.strongAnswer:
Concrete example: thread A locks account then ledger, while thread B locks ledger then account; each can block forever waiting for the other.
A separate race happens when two threads do count++ or check-then-act without synchronization, because read-modify-write is not atomic.
Use consistent lock ordering, atomic classes or synchronized blocks for compound updates, and avoid holding locks during I/O.
evaluations.followUpQuestion: Can you elaborate further and give a concrete example?

evaluations.question: How would you explain java concurrency to a junior developer who has never seen it?
evaluations.answer: Java concurrency is complex, you need to read the fundamentals, start simple examples, read about deadlocks, race condition, then study the Java modern library
evaluations.strongAnswer:
Explain it as many workers using the same kitchen: concurrency lets several tasks make progress at once, but shared tools must be coordinated.
In Java, threads can share memory, so bugs happen when two threads read or change the same data without the right rules.
Start juniors with ExecutorService, synchronized, and concurrent collections before lower-level lock tuning.
evaluations.followUpQuestion: Can you elaborate further and give a concrete example?

evaluations.question: If you had to use java concurrency in a high-scale production system, what tradeoffs would you consider?
evaluations.answer: using threads add more complexity, dealing with async code is not as simple as sync code, but we need threads in a high-scale system, threads make it possible your system to execute tasks seemingly, for example it can handle different tasks like calling an API and the main thread wont be blocked.
evaluations.strongAnswer:
At scale, concurrency improves throughput and latency hiding, but it raises operational risk through contention, queue growth, context switching, and harder debugging.
The key tradeoffs are pool sizing, bounded versus unbounded queues, backpressure, and whether tasks are CPU-bound or mostly waiting on I/O.
Use bounded executors, measure saturation, isolate critical workloads, and prefer simpler designs when parallelism does not clearly improve the bottleneck.
evaluations.feedback: Adequate, but could go deeper on key points.
evaluations.followUpQuestion: Can you elaborate further and give a concrete example?

questions: Walk me through what happens at the OS and JVM level when you run `java -jar app.jar`.
How many threads exist in a JVM process before your `main()` method executes, and what types are they?
Your production Java service has 400 threads, high CPU, and slow responses. How do you diagnose and interpret a thread dump?
A thread dump shows 200 threads BLOCKED on the same lock. Walk me through how you identify the root cause and fix it.
How do heap dumps and thread dumps complement each other during a production incident?
seed-java-os-jvm-internals.summary: Seeded session from `java-os-jvm.md`. Focus: JVM startup, internal JVM threads, thread dumps, lock contention, heap-vs-thread diagnostics, and practical production debugging.

questions: What is the difference between TLS and mTLS, and when would you choose mTLS?
